items.title: Drug counsellor who delivered fatal ketamine doses to Matthew Perry gets 2 years in prison
items.summary_auto: A licensed drug addiction counsellor who delivered Friends star Matthew Perry the doses of ketamine that killed him receivd his sentence on Wednesday.

items.title: Family of former Olympic skater killed in crash gives emotional victim impact statements in court
items.summary_auto: The family of a former Canadian Olympic figure skater killed in a 2023 car crash gave emotional victim impact statements in an Ontario court on Wednesday, as a judge prepares to sentence the man who pleaded guilty to dangerous driving charges.

items.title: Federal, Manitoba governments breached First Nations' child welfare rights, judge says in 'historic' decision
items.summary_auto: In a decision described as "historic," a Manitoba judge has found the governments of Manitoba and Canada breached First Nations' rights to self-governance around child welfare.

items.title: Navy Commander Angus Topshee promoted to vice chief of defence staff
items.summary_auto: Vice-Admiral Angus Topshee, the head of the Royal Canadian Navy, is being promoted to second-in-command of the Canadian Armed Forces.

items.title: Will Canada compete in Eurovision? We want to know
items.summary_auto: Eurovision director Martin Green says Canada's entry is possible but no request or decision has been made. Any participation would need European Broadcasting Union (EBU) approval. CBC/Radio‑Canada is an associate EBU member and not currently eligible to compete. However, the public broadcaster is sending observers to this year's Eurovision Song Contest while talks continue.

items.title: Cycling Canada is cutting women's pursuit team. These Canadian riders have launched an appeal
items.summary_auto: Five Canadian cyclists are prepared to fight back after it was announced Cycling Canada would not be sending a women’s pursuit team to this year’s world championships slated for Oct. 14-18.

items.title: Facing mounting backlash, Anandasangaree says U.S. tech companies are 'misinterpreting' his lawful access bill
items.summary_auto: Public Safety Minister Gary Anandasangaree accused U.S. tech giants of "misinterpreting" his lawful access bill, which promises to give police and spies faster access to Canadians' information during investigations, as the proposed legislation faces growing backlash south of the border.

items.title: 'I wish I could turn back time': Ex-Ontario police sergeant gets prison for obstruction, breach of trust
items.summary_auto: A former staff sergeant with the Thunder Bay Police Service (TBPS) has been sentenced to three years in prison after being found guilty of breach of trust and obstruction of justice. Here's more about the sentencing of Michael Dimini in the northwestern Ontario city.

items.title: Ottawa, Alberta near deal on carbon pricing rising to $130/tonne by 2040
items.summary_auto: The federal government and the Alberta government are nailing down a deal on industrial carbon pricing, largely considered Canada's most important policy for driving down harmful greenhouse gas pollution.

items.title: Gunshots in Philippine Senate as politician wanted by ICC urges people to mobilize
items.summary_auto: Gunshots broke out at the Philippine Senate on Wednesday and people ran for cover after a top politician wanted by the International Criminal Court said his arrest was imminent and security forces entered the building.

items.title: From UFOs to missing scientists, has Trump made conspiracy theories mainstream?
items.summary_auto: From UFOs to missing scientists, fringe ideas and conspiracy theories have come to the forefront during U.S. President Donald Trump’s second administration. This week, Washington correspondents Katie Simpson, Paul Hunter and Willy Lowry dig into the long history of conspiracy theories intersecting with American politics.

items.title: Trump's dismissal of Americans' inflation concerns fuels Democrats' midterm campaigns
items.summary_auto: U.S. President Donald Trump said on Tuesday he isn't thinking about the financial situation of Americans as he tries to navigate a path toward a ceasefire with Iran, as the economic impacts of the conflict that began in late February start to become more apparent in the U.S.

items.title: Carney tells Artemis II astronauts their mission around the moon inspired a country
items.summary_auto: Prime Minister Mark Carney welcomed the Artemis II astronauts to Ottawa Wednesday telling them their recent trip around the moon has served as an inspiration for the whole country.

items.title: PWHL to add teams in Hamilton and Las Vegas ahead of next season
items.summary_auto: The PWHL will add expansion teams in Hamilton and Las Vegas ahead of next season, the latest step in the league's rapid expansion plan. The league announced the two cities on Wednesday.

items.title: AI transcriber for use by Ontario doctors 'hallucinated,' generated errors, auditor finds
Mother charged with murder in death of 1-year-old girl: Toronto police
items.summary_auto: The mother of a one-year-old girl has been charged with first-degree murder after the child died in hospital Wednesday, Toronto police say.

items.title: Why a song contest has emerged as Europe’s most controversial election
items.summary_auto: The 2026 Eurovision Song Contest has devolved into its worst crisis in history, marked by a historic five-country boycott and audience protests over Israel’s participation amid the war in Gaza.

items.title: Board of Peace envoy says stalled ceasefire hinges on disarmament of Hamas
items.summary_auto: The top diplomat overseeing the U.S.-brokered ceasefire in Gaza, said Wednesday the truce hinged on Hamas' disarmament, a sticking point that has stalled progress on other fronts, including rebuilding the mostly destroyed enclave.

items.title: The science behind B.C. Ferries weather cancellations
items.summary_auto: The process behind deciding to cancel a ferry sailing because of weather is a high stakes calculation that involves a high-tech nerve center, a 28-year Navy veteran and a strict mathematical matrix designed to survive the volatile waters of the Salish Sea.

items.title: Are Canadians reaching their 'breaking point'? New data shows more people filing for insolvency
The Tragically Hip's final 2016 concert set to re-air on CBC this summer
items.summary_auto: A new live album to commemorate the band's Man Machine Poem tour is also set for release in August.

items.title: A disabled man living in a group home died malnourished in hospital. His family wants answers
items.summary_auto: A family is calling for an inquest after an intellectually disabled man who was being cared for at a Manitoba group home died in hospital with significant malnutrition.

items.title: N.B. affordable housing waitlist hits record 14,000 households amid spur of new construction
items.summary_auto: There are new public housing units under construction across the province for the first time in decades. But the increase in public housing stock won't last long, with a record 14,000 households waiting in line for one of those units.

items.title: No apology included in proposed $30M settlement for Christian Brothers abuse case
items.summary_auto: Some former students of Vancouver College say a proposed $30-million settlement is meaningless without an apology and an admission of liability for the abuse they allegedly suffered at the hands of Christian Brothers linked to the Mount Cashel Orphanage.

items.title: Canada's new asylum law leaves 2SLGBTQ+ claimants fearing deportation
items.summary_auto: Ahmed came to Canada as an international student planning to return home — until falling in love with another man forced him to confront the dangers he says he faces in Pakistan. Now, a new federal asylum law could make his refugee claim ineligible before he even got a hearing.

items.title: 3rd-party fraud, security risks flagged in some Canadian visa hubs abroad: internal records
items.summary_auto: An Ottawa man is among critics urging stronger oversight of Canada's international visa hubs — run by a federal contractor paid hundreds of millions taxpayer dollars — after what he describes as "unjustifiable" and "shady" experiences at a visa centre in Bangladesh.

items.title: Two pedestrians struck, killed by trains in Thunder Bay, Ont., in less than 2 weeks
items.summary_auto: The Thunder Bay Police Service (TBPS) has confirmed a second train-related death involving a pedestrian in the city in less than a two-week period. Here's what we know about the incidents, as well as tips shared on railway safety.

items.title: Is there a Diet Coke shortage? What to know about India's 'candemic'
items.summary_auto: A scarcity of aluminum cans caused by the Iran war means there's a Diet Coke shortage in India, where the drink only comes in cans. As a result, people there are hoarding the beverages and throwing parties to celebrate their favourite drink.

items.title: Fentanyl ingredients entering Canada via Vancouver en route to cartel-run drug labs, U.S. DEA boss says
items.summary_auto: Chemicals used to make fentanyl are streaming into the Port of Vancouver on their way to drug labs run by Mexican cartels on Canadian soil, the head of the U.S. Drug Enforcement Administration told senators in Washington, D.C., on Tuesday.

items.title: The Canadian inspiration for Britain's surging right-wing Reform party
items.summary_auto: The ascendant Reform UK leader says Preston Manning, the wonkish Canadian populist pioneer, is his inspiration.

items.title: Obsession — and the internet — is about to change the face of horror
items.summary_auto: We shouldn't be surprised that some of the best horror in 2026 is coming from a very online sketch comedian. Obsession, from 26-year-old Curry Barker, is about to change horror for good.

items.title: Sexual violence was 'deliberate tactic' and integral to Hamas-led Oct. 7 attacks, report finds
items.summary_auto: Sexual violence was systematic, widespread and integral to the Hamas-led Oct. 7 attacks and their aftermath, a new report by an Israeli non-profit has found.

items.title: A hormonal condition that affects tens of millions of women is being renamed. Here's why that's important
items.summary_auto: After more than a decade of discussion and debate, polycystic ovary syndrome, or PCOS, will now be called polyendocrine metabolic ovarian syndrome, or PMOS. It's hoped the name change will mean earlier diagnosis, an approach toward holistic treatments and more awareness of lesser known symptoms of the chronic hormonal disorder.

items.title: PCOS, which affects tens of millions of women, is now known as PMOS. Here's why the renaming is important
items.summary_auto: After more than a decade of discussion and debate, polycystic ovary syndrome, or PCOS, will now be called polyendocrine metabolic ovarian syndrome, or PMOS. It's hoped the name change will mean earlier diagnosis, an approach toward holistic treatments and more awareness of lesser known symptoms of the chronic hormonal disorder.
